# Gemini Review Pass 2: Language Pedant

> **You are a Senior Ukrainian Language Editor reviewing curriculum content.**
> Your task: evaluate language quality, naturalness, pedagogical flow, and known error patterns.
> You do NOT check factual accuracy against sources — that is a separate reviewer's job.

**IMPORTANT: This content was generated by another AI agent in a separate session.
You are an independent reviewer. Evaluate purely on quality.
Do not assume you wrote this content. Do not be generous.**

---

## Module Content

```markdown
## Вступ (Introduction)

Welcome to one of the most powerful patterns in Ukrainian! Every noun you've learned so far — **ма́ма**, **та́то**, **кіт**, **мі́сто** — carries a hidden tag: its gender. Ukrainian has three genders: Masculine, Feminine, and Neuter. This isn't about biology — it applies to every noun, from people to places to everyday objects. Your **стіл** (table) is masculine. Your **кни́га** (book) is feminine. Your **вікно́** (window) is neuter.

Here's the good news: you can predict the gender correctly about 95% of the time just by looking at a word's ending. You already have the tools — you just need the code.

### The Neuter Sun

In many European languages, the sun is masculine — *el sol* in Spanish, *le soleil* in French. But in Ukrainian, **со́нце** (sun) is neuter — an impartial, gentle life-giver that shines equally on everyone. This reflects something meaningful about Ukrainian: neuter isn't «less than» — it's a category of balance.

### Your Color Code

Think of three colors to keep the genders straight:

- 🔵 **Blue = Masculine** — hard endings, consonants
- 🔴 **Red = Feminine** — open endings, **-а** / **-я**
- 🟡 **Yellow = Neuter** — round endings, **-о** / **-е**

Keep this code in mind — you'll see these patterns everywhere.

> [!tip] Quick Win
> You already know words from all three genders! **Дім** = masculine. **Мама** = feminine. **Місто** = neuter. You've been reading gendered nouns since Module 1!

---

## Презентація правил (Presentation of Rules)

### Ending Patterns

The ending of a noun is your best clue to its gender. Here are the three main patterns:

**🔵 Masculine — ends in a consonant:**

- **стіл** (table) — ends in **-л**
- **хліб** (bread) — ends in **-б**
- **дім** (house) — ends in **-м**
- **брат** (brother) — ends in **-т**

**🔴 Feminine — ends in -а or -я:**

- **кни́га** (book) — ends in **-а**
- **земля́** (earth, land) — ends in **-я**
- **зона** (zone) — ends in **-а**
- **соба́ка** (dog) — ends in **-а**

**🟡 Neuter — ends in -о or -е:**

- **вікно́** (window) — ends in **-о**
- **мі́сто** (city) — ends in **-о**
- **мо́ре** (sea) — ends in **-е**
- **се́рце** (heart) — ends in **-е**

### The Diagnostic Tool: мій, моя, моє

Ukrainian textbooks teach a simple test. Try placing **мій** (my — masculine), **моя́** (my — feminine), or **моє́** (my — neuter) before the noun. The one that sounds right tells you the gender.

<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 3, p. 110 -->

| Masculine (він, мій) | Feminine (вона, моя) | Neuter (воно, моє) |
|---|---|---|
| мій **стіл** | моя **кни́га** | моє **вікно́** |
| мій **дім** | моя **ма́ма** | моє **мі́сто** |
| мій **брат** | моя **сестра́** | моє **мо́ре** |
| мій **день** | моя **земля́** | моє **се́рце** |

This is your primary tool. When you're unsure about gender, ask yourself: **мій, моя́, or моє́**?

### Agreement — Adjectives Follow the Noun

Gender affects more than pronouns. Adjectives change their endings to match the noun. Watch how **великий** (big) transforms:

- **вели́кий стіл** — masculine ending **-ий**
- **цікáва кни́га** — feminine ending **-а**
- **чи́сте вікно́** — neuter ending **-е**

You don't need to memorize all adjective forms right now. Just notice the pattern: the adjective ending matches the noun's gender.

### Family Words in Action

Gender becomes natural with family vocabulary. Notice how some family nouns match the person's biological gender:

- **Мій брат** — my brother (masculine word, male person ✓)
- **Моя́ сестра́** — my sister (feminine word, female person ✓)
- **Моя́ ма́ма** — my mother (feminine word, female person ✓)

> **(At home / Вдома)**
> — Це мій **брат**.
> — А це моя **сестра́**.
> — Моя **ма́ма** тут?
> — Так, **ма́ма** тут.

<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 3, p. 110 -->

> [!note] Remember
> You already know **моя** and **моє** from earlier modules. Now you're adding **мій** — the masculine form. Together, these three words are your gender detectives.

> **(In a room / У кімнаті)**
> — Це мій **стіл**.
> — А це моя **кни́га**.
> — **Вікно́** вели́ке!
> — Так, моє **вікно́** вели́ке.

---

## Практичні вправи (Practice Exercises)

### The Тато Trap

Here's your first exception. The word **та́то** (father) ends in **-о**, which should make it neuter. But **тато** refers to a male person, so biological gender overrides the ending pattern.

- **Мій та́то** ✓ (correct — masculine, because he's male)
- ~~Моє тато~~ ✗ (wrong — this would mean «my neuter father»)

Compare:

- **Мій та́то** — my dad (masculine — real person)
- **Моє мі́сто** — my city (neuter — thing)

Same **-о** ending, different genders. When a noun names a real person, biology wins.

> [!warning] Watch Out!
> The **-о** ending usually means neuter — but NOT for people. **Та́то** = мій тато (masculine). **Мі́сто** = моє місто (neuter). Always check: does the word refer to a person?

### The Soft Sign Mystery: день vs. ніч

Some nouns end in a soft sign (**ь**), which gives you no ending clue at all. You have to memorize these. Here's the most useful pair:

- **Мій день** — my day (masculine)
- **Моя ніч** — my night (feminine)

A helpful trick: learn them as opposites. The adjective endings will remind you of the gender every time you use them:

- **Га́рний день** (good/nice day) — masculine **-ий**
- **До́бра ніч** (good night) — feminine **-а**

> **(Greeting / Привітання)**
> — Га́рний **день**!
> — Дякую! Га́рний **день**!
> — До́бра **ніч**, **ма́мо**!
> — До́бра **ніч**, си́ну!

> [!practice] Try It
> Say these pairs aloud. Notice how the adjective changes:
> - **гарний день** — **добрий день**
> - **мій день** — **моя ніч**

### The Name Trap: ім'я

The word **ім'я́** (name) ends in **-я**, which looks feminine — like **земля́** (earth). But **ім'я́** is actually neuter! It belongs to a small special group of nouns.

Compare these two carefully:

- **Моя земля́** — my earth (feminine — regular **-я** word)
- **Моє ім'я́** — my name (neuter — exception!)

How to remember: **ім'я** uses **моє**, just like **вікно** and **місто**. When in doubt, the pronoun test always works.

### Sorting Drill

Try sorting each noun before reading the answer:

<!-- adapted from: Ponomarova, Grade 4, p. 35 -->

- **чолові́к** (man) → мій чоловік — masculine ✓
- **жі́нка** (woman) → моя жінка — feminine ✓
- **мі́сто** (city) → моє місто — neuter ✓
- **со́нце** (sun) → моє сонце — neuter ✓

---

## Самостійна робота (Independent Work/Production)

### The «It» Trap

In English, you call most objects «it.» In Ukrainian, there is no single «it» — every noun is **він** (he/it — masculine), **вона́** (she/it — feminine), or **воно́** (it — neuter).

Don't fall into the trap of using **воно́** for everything! Practice:

- **Стіл** → він (not воно!)
- **Кни́га** → вона (not воно!)
- **Вікно́** → воно ✓

Your table is «він.» Your book is «вона.» Only your window is «воно.»

### The S.T.A.L.K.E.R. Code

If you know the Ukrainian-developed game S.T.A.L.K.E.R., you already have three perfect gender anchors:

- 🔵 **артефа́кт** (artifact) — consonant ending → мій артефакт (masculine)
- 🔴 **Зона** (zone) — **-а** ending → моя зона (feminine)
- 🟡 **укриття́** (shelter) — neuter exception in **-я**, like **ім'я** → моє укриття (neuter)

Even without the game, these three words make a handy shortcut — one from each gender, easy to remember.

### Build Your Phrases

Now try creating simple phrases with **мій/моя/моє** and the adjectives you've seen:

- **Мій нови́й дім** — my new house
- **Моя́ цікáва кни́га** — my interesting book
- **Моє вели́ке вікно́** — my big window
- **Мій старший брат** — my older brother
- **Моя́ моло́дша сестра́** — my younger sister

You can build your own phrases by combining any noun with **мій/моя/моє**. The pronoun must match the noun's gender — that's agreement in action.

> **(Describing things / Опис)**
> — Це мій нови́й **дім**.
> — **Дім** вели́кий?
> — Так! І моє **вікно́** вели́ке.
> — А моя **кни́га** цікáва!

> [!challenge] Your Turn
> Look around your room. Pick three objects. Can you say **мій**, **моя**, or **моє** for each? If you know the Ukrainian word, try the full phrase!

---

## Культурний код та підсумок (Cultural Code and Summary)

You've cracked the gender code! Here's what you now know:

**The 95% Rule** — most nouns follow predictable patterns:

- Consonant ending → masculine (**дім**, **брат**, **стіл**)
- **-а/-я** ending → feminine (**кни́га**, **ма́ма**, **земля́**)
- **-о/-е** ending → neuter (**вікно́**, **мі́сто**, **мо́ре**)

**The exceptions** to watch for:

- People override endings: **та́то** = мій тато (masculine)
- Soft sign (**ь**) nouns need memorizing: **день** (M) vs. **ніч** (F)
- Special neuters in **-я**: **ім'я́** (моє ім'я), **укри́ття** (моє укриття)

### How Gender Shapes the Ukrainian World

Ukrainian doesn't just label words — it brings them to life. The earth is **земля́** — feminine, like a mother. Ukrainians say **земля́-ма́ти** (mother earth). The sun is **со́нце** — neuter, an impartial life-giver: **я́сне со́нце** (bright sun). The heart is **се́рце** — also neuter, belonging to everyone equally: **до́бре се́рце** (kind heart).

These aren't random assignments. They reflect how Ukrainian speakers see the world — and now you're starting to see it that way too.

### Your Competency Check

Can you identify the gender of these core words? Cover the answers and test yourself:

- **ім'я́** → ? (neuter — моє ім'я)
- **та́то** → ? (masculine — мій тато)
- **ма́ма** → ? (feminine — моя мама)
- **мі́сто** → ? (neuter — моє місто)

If you got all four right — including the tricky ones — great work! You've built a solid foundation for everything that comes next.
# Summary

In this module, you've learned the fundamental patterns of Ukrainian noun gender. Remember the color code: **Blue (Masculine)** for consonants, **Red (Feminine)** for -а/-я, and **Yellow (Neuter)** for -о/-е. While there are some tricky words like **тато** or **ім'я**, the 95% rule will serve you well. Keep practicing with **мій**, **моя**, and **моє** to build your intuition!

```

## Activities

```yaml
- type: match-up
  title: Sort Nouns by Gender
  instruction: Match each Ukrainian noun to its grammatical gender.
  pairs:
  - left: стіл
    right: Masculine
  - left: книга
    right: Feminine
  - left: вікно
    right: Neuter
  - left: брат
    right: Masculine
  - left: земля
    right: Feminine
  - left: море
    right: Neuter
  - left: собака
    right: Feminine
  - left: серце
    right: Neuter
  - left: хліб
    right: Masculine
  - left: сестра
    right: Feminine
- type: quiz
  title: Identify Gender by Ending
  instruction: Choose the correct gender based on the word's ending.
  items:
  - question: What gender is дім (house)? It ends in a consonant.
    explanation: Nouns ending in a consonant are masculine.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: true
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is книга (book)? It ends in -а.
    explanation: Nouns ending in -а are feminine.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Feminine
      correct: true
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is вікно (window)? It ends in -о.
    explanation: Nouns ending in -о are neuter.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: true
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is тато (dad)? Be careful — it ends in -о but refers to
      a male person.
    explanation: Тато ends in -о, but because it refers to a male person, biological
      gender overrides the ending. It is masculine.
    options:
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: Masculine
      correct: true
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is море (sea)? It ends in -е.
    explanation: Nouns ending in -е are neuter.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: true
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is земля (earth)? It ends in -я.
    explanation: Nouns ending in -я are usually feminine.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Feminine
      correct: true
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is день (day)? It ends in a soft sign (ь).
    explanation: День is masculine. Soft sign nouns must be memorized — день is masculine,
      but ніч (night) is feminine.
    options:
    - text: Masculine
      correct: true
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: false
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
  - question: What gender is ім'я (name)? It ends in -я, but it is a special exception.
    explanation: Ім'я looks feminine because of -я, but it is actually neuter — моє
      ім'я.
    options:
    - text: Feminine
      correct: false
    - text: Masculine
      correct: false
    - text: Neuter
      correct: true
    - text: No gender
      correct: false
- type: fill-in
  title: Choose the Correct Possessive
  instruction: Pick the right form of 'my' for each noun.
  items:
  - sentence: ___ стіл (table)
    answer: мій
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Стіл is masculine, so we use мій.
  - sentence: ___ книга (book)
    answer: моя
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Книга is feminine, so we use моя.
  - sentence: ___ вікно (window)
    answer: моє
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Вікно is neuter, so we use моє.
  - sentence: ___ тато (dad)
    answer: мій
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Тато refers to a male person, so it is masculine — мій тато.
  - sentence: ___ сестра (sister)
    answer: моя
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Сестра is feminine, so we use моя.
  - sentence: ___ місто (city)
    answer: моє
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Місто is neuter, so we use моє.
  - sentence: ___ брат (brother)
    answer: мій
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Брат is masculine, so we use мій.
  - sentence: ___ ім'я (name)
    answer: моє
    options:
    - мій
    - моя
    - моє
    - мої
    explanation: Ім'я is a neuter exception — моє ім'я.
- type: match-up
  title: Noun to Possessive Pronoun
  instruction: Match each noun with the correct possessive pronoun.
  pairs:
  - left: дім
    right: мій
  - left: мама
    right: моя
  - left: сонце
    right: моє
  - left: собака
    right: моя
  - left: артефакт
    right: мій
  - left: зона
    right: моя
  - left: укриття
    right: моє
  - left: серце
    right: моє
- type: group-sort
  title: Sort by Gender
  instruction: Place each noun into the correct gender group.
  groups:
  - name: Masculine (мій)
    items:
    - стіл
    - дім
    - брат
    - хліб
  - name: Feminine (моя)
    items:
    - книга
    - мама
    - сестра
    - земля
  - name: Neuter (моє)
    items:
    - вікно
    - місто
    - море
    - сонце
- type: true-false
  title: True or False?
  instruction: Decide whether each statement about Ukrainian gender is true or false.
  items:
  - statement: Nouns ending in a consonant are usually masculine.
    correct: true
    explanation: Correct! Consonant endings like стіл, дім, брат signal masculine
      gender.
  - statement: Nouns ending in -а or -я are always feminine.
    correct: false
    explanation: Usually yes, but there are exceptions. Ім'я ends in -я but is neuter.
  - statement: The word тато is neuter because it ends in -о.
    correct: false
    explanation: Тато refers to a male person, so biological gender overrides the
      ending — it is masculine.
  - statement: Моя is the feminine form of 'my' in Ukrainian.
    correct: true
    explanation: Correct! Мій (masculine), моя (feminine), моє (neuter).
  - statement: The word ніч (night) is masculine.
    correct: false
    explanation: Ніч is feminine — добра ніч (good night). Soft sign nouns need to
      be memorized.
  - statement: Вікно is neuter because it ends in -о.
    correct: true
    explanation: Correct! The -о ending signals neuter gender.
  - statement: In Ukrainian, every noun has a grammatical gender.
    correct: true
    explanation: Correct! Ukrainian has three genders — masculine, feminine, and neuter
      — for all nouns.
  - statement: The word день (day) is feminine.
    correct: false
    explanation: День is masculine — мій день, гарний день.
- type: unjumble
  title: Put the Words in Order
  instruction: Arrange the words to form a correct Ukrainian phrase.
  items:
  - words:
    - стіл
    - Це
    - мій
    answer: Це мій стіл
  - words:
    - книга
    - моя
    - Це
    answer: Це моя книга
  - words:
    - вікно
    - моє
    - Це
    answer: Це моє вікно
  - words:
    - брат
    - мій
    - Це
    answer: Це мій брат
  - words:
    - сестра
    - моя
    - Це
    answer: Це моя сестра
  - words:
    - місто
    - моє
    - Це
    answer: Це моє місто
- type: anagram
  title: Unscramble the Word
  instruction: Rearrange the letters to form a Ukrainian word from the lesson.
  items:
  - scrambled: г а н и к
    answer: книга
  - scrambled: і с т л
    answer: стіл
  - scrambled: о н і к в
    answer: вікно
  - scrambled: р е м о
    answer: море
  - scrambled: р а б т
    answer: брат
  - scrambled: ц е р с е
    answer: серце

```

## Vocabulary

```yaml
items:
  - lemma: "брат"
    translation: "brother"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "m"
    usage: "старший брат (older brother)"
    example: "Це мій брат."
  - lemma: "сестра"
    translation: "sister"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "f"
    usage: "молодша сестра (younger sister)"
    example: "Це моя сестра."
  - lemma: "мама"
    translation: "mother, mom"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "f"
    usage: "люба мама (dear mom)"
    example: "Моя мама тут."
  - lemma: "тато"
    translation: "father, dad"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "m"
    notes: "Ends in -о but masculine — biological gender overrides ending"
    example: "Це мій тато."
  - lemma: "дім"
    translation: "house, home"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "m"
    usage: "новий дім (new house)"
    example: "Це мій дім."
  - lemma: "вікно"
    translation: "window"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "n"
    usage: "велике вікно (big window)"
    example: "Моє вікно велике."
  - lemma: "книга"
    translation: "book"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "f"
    usage: "цікава книга (interesting book)"
    example: "Це моя книга."
  - lemma: "місто"
    translation: "city"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "n"
    usage: "велике місто (big city)"
    example: "Моє місто велике."
  - lemma: "стіл"
    translation: "table"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "m"
    usage: "великий стіл (big table)"
    example: "Це мій стіл."
  - lemma: "море"
    translation: "sea"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "n"
    usage: "синє море (blue sea)"
    example: "Моє море тепле."
  - lemma: "ніч"
    translation: "night"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "f"
    notes: "Feminine despite soft sign ending — memorize as pair with день"
    example: "Добра ніч!"
  - lemma: "день"
    translation: "day"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "m"
    notes: "Masculine despite soft sign ending — memorize as pair with ніч"
    example: "Гарний день!"
  - lemma: "земля"
    translation: "earth, land"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "f"
    usage: "рідна земля (native land)"
    example: "Моя земля."
  - lemma: "серце"
    translation: "heart"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "n"
    usage: "добре серце (kind heart)"
    example: "Моє серце."
  - lemma: "сонце"
    translation: "sun"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "n"
    notes: "Neuter — cultural symbol as impartial life-giver"
    example: "Ясне сонце."
  - lemma: "собака"
    translation: "dog"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "f"
    example: "Моя собака тут."
  - lemma: "ім'я"
    translation: "name"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "n"
    notes: "Neuter exception — ends in -я but uses моє, not моя"
    example: "Моє ім'я."
  - lemma: "артефакт"
    translation: "artifact"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "m"
    notes: "S.T.A.L.K.E.R. vocabulary — consonant ending, masculine"
    example: "Мій артефакт."
  - lemma: "зона"
    translation: "zone"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "f"
    notes: "S.T.A.L.K.E.R. vocabulary — -а ending, feminine"
    example: "Моя зона."
  - lemma: "укриття"
    translation: "shelter"
    pos: "noun"
    gender: "n"
    notes: "S.T.A.L.K.E.R. vocabulary — neuter exception ending in -я"
    example: "Моє укриття."
```

## Content Sections (H2 headers)

1. Вступ (Introduction)
2. Презентація правил (Presentation of Rules)
3. Практичні вправи (Practice Exercises)
4. Самостійна робота (Independent Work/Production)
5. Культурний код та підсумок (Cultural Code and Summary)

**Section Reference Rule:** When discussing content sections, reference them by their **exact Ukrainian H2 header name**.

---

## Friction Constraints (Past Review Findings)

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.

---

## Tier-Specific Guidance

# Track Calibration: A1

## Bilingual Scope
A1 uses PROGRESSIVE immersion — targets increase per module:
- Modules 1-2: 5-15% Ukrainian (Cyrillic intro, mostly English)
- Modules 3-5: 10-25% Ukrainian (early vocab building)
- Modules 6-10: 15-35% Ukrainian (growing immersion)
- Modules 11-20: 25-45% Ukrainian (foundation established)
- Modules 21+: 30-55% Ukrainian (consolidation)

Mixing English explanations with Ukrainian examples is CORRECT pedagogy.
Do NOT flag bilingual content as LANGUAGE_BLENDER.
Flag: Full Ukrainian paragraphs that exceed the module's immersion band.
Flag: Modules that are below their minimum immersion target.

## Russicism Lookup (A1-specific)
These appear frequently in A1 content. Flag as HIGH:
- здача → решта (change/money)
- тапочки → капці (slippers)
- кушати → їсти (to eat)
- давайте попрактикуємо → попрактикуймо (let's practice — Russian imperative calque)
- давайте повторимо → повторімо (let's repeat — Russian imperative calque)
- давайте подивимося → подивімося (let's look — Russian imperative calque)
- чоловіче → пане (sir — register mismatch in service contexts)
- надіятися → сподіватися (to hope)
- піднімається → підводиться (gets up)
- получати → отримувати (to receive)
- вообще → взагалі (in general)

## Anglicism Lookup (A1-specific)
- "Що ви хочете?" → "Що бажаєте?" (register in service/hospitality contexts)
- "роблять каву" → "готують каву" (make coffee — English calque)
- "робити добру каву" → "готувати смачну каву" (make good coffee)

## LLM Filler Sensitivity
At A1, some motivational content is ACCEPTABLE when woven into teaching.
Flag ONLY: pure cheerleading with zero educational content, generic padding
("Numbers are everywhere", "Language is not just about labeling things",
"As you continue your Ukrainian journey").
Do NOT flag: warm encouragement that includes a teaching point.

## Content Focus
Simple sentences are expected. Don't flag short paragraphs.
Focus on: Russianisms, factual errors in callouts, and fluff replacing actual teaching.
Do NOT penalize: friendly tone, bilingual explanations, basic vocabulary presentation.

   Fix: Replace 'я́сне' with 'я́сне́'.

## Russianisms Checklist

These appear frequently in A1 content. Flag as HIGH:
- здача → решта (change/money)
- тапочки → капці (slippers)
- кушати → їсти (to eat)
- давайте попрактикуємо → попрактикуймо (let's practice — Russian imperative calque)
- давайте повторимо → повторімо (let's repeat — Russian imperative calque)
- давайте подивимося → подивімося (let's look — Russian imperative calque)
- чоловіче → пане (sir — register mismatch in service contexts)
- надіятися → сподіватися (to hope)
- піднімається → підводиться (gets up)
- получати → отримувати (to receive)
- вообще → взагалі (in general)

## VESUM Word Verification

**Words checked:** 47 | **VESUM coverage:** 45/47 (95.7%)

**❌ Not found in VESUM or textbooks (2):**
- `земля-мати` (source: prose)
- `ий` (source: prose)

**Action:** Check if these are valid Ukrainian word forms. Proper nouns and vocative forms may be legitimate. Hallucinated forms or Russianisms must be flagged.

---

## Review Protocol

### STEP 1: Language Quality Scan
- Every Ukrainian sentence: grammar, case endings, verb aspects, preposition agreement
- Russianisms: check against the checklist above AND scan for unlisted ones
- Calques: робити сенс→мати сенс, брати місце→відбуватися, etc.
- Natural Ukrainian phrasing vs translated-from-English constructions
- VESUM failures from pre-screen: are they valid forms or errors?

### STEP 2: Pedagogical Flow
- Does the lesson build progressively (simple → complex)?
- Are examples relevant to the grammar/vocabulary being taught?
- Would a beginner understand each step? ("Would I Continue?" test)
- Are activities well-designed and error-free?

### STEP 3: Auto-Fail Checklist
- Russianisms (кушать→їсти, приймати участь→брати участь, etc.)
- Colonial framing (Ukrainian defined by contrast with Russian)
- Word salad (unrelated claims strung together)
- Grammar scope violations (teaching concepts from later modules)
- Activity errors (wrong answers, impossible tasks)

### STEP 3a: Colonial Framing Check
Flag: "Unlike Russian...", "Different from Russian...", defining Ukrainian via Russian's absence.
Exceptions: `[!myth-buster]` debunking propaganda, `[!decolonization]` blocks, historical context.

### STEP 3b: LLM Fingerprint Scan
- Structural monotony: do 3+ sections start the same way?
- Example batching: 3+ sections with identical example formatting?
- Generic AI rhetoric: "це не просто", stacked abstract nouns, AI clichés
- Callout monotony: 3+ callouts with the same title?

### STEP 4: Score 7 Dimensions

| # | Dimension | Weight | Auto-fail |
|---|-----------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Experience Quality | 1.5 | <7 |
| 2 | Language | 1.1 | <8 |
| 3 | Pedagogy | 1.2 | <7 |
| 4 | Activities | 1.3 | <7 |
| 5 | Beginner Safety | 1.3 | <7 |
| 6 | LLM Fingerprint | 1.0 | <7 |
| 7 | Linguistic Accuracy | 1.5 | <9 |

**Weighted Overall:**
```
Overall = (Experience x 1.5 + Language x 1.1 + Pedagogy x 1.2 +
          Activities x 1.3 + Beginner_Safety x 1.3 + LLM x 1.0 +
          Linguistic_Accuracy x 1.5) / 8.9
```

**Why 7 dimensions?** A1/A2 modules are short and topic-constrained, so Coherence, Relevance, Educational, Immersion, Richness, and Factual Accuracy are noise at this level — they auto-pass trivially and waste reviewer attention. Focus scoring on what actually differentiates good beginner modules.

---

## MINIMUM REQUIREMENTS

Your review MUST contain:
1. **>=3 real issues** with specific locations and quoted Ukrainian text
2. **>=2 dimensions scored below 9** (no module is perfect)
3. **Specific evidence** for every score
4. **Fix Plan** for any dimension below 9

---

## ANTI-GAMING ENFORCEMENT

**Triggers AUTOMATIC REJECTION:**
1. Gaming language ("ensuring a high score", "designed to pass")
2. All scores >= 9/10 with no issues
3. Praise-only citations
4. Fabricated citations

---

## REVIEWER IDENTIFICATION

Your review MUST start with:
```
**Reviewed-By:** gemini-2.5-pro (RAG-grounded)
```

## Output Format

Use these exact delimiters:

```
===REVIEW_START===

**Status:** PASS / FAIL
**Overall Score:** X/10

## Scores

| # | Dimension | Score | Auto-fail | Evidence |
|---|-----------|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Experience Quality | X/10 | <7 | [specific finding] |
| 2 | Language | X/10 | <8 | [specific finding] |
| 3 | Pedagogy | X/10 | <7 | [specific finding] |
| 4 | Activities | X/10 | <7 | [specific finding] |
| 5 | Beginner Safety | X/10 | <7 | ["Would I Continue?" X/5] |
| 6 | LLM Fingerprint | X/10 | <7 | [specific finding] |
| 7 | Linguistic Accuracy | X/10 | <9 | [specific finding] |

**Weighted Overall:** ... = **X.X/10**

## Critical Issues Found

### Issue 1: [title]
**Location**: [section or line reference]
**Problem**: [specific description with quoted Ukrainian text]
**Fix**: [concrete fix]

[... more issues ...]

## Ukrainian Language Issues

[Specific language problems found with quoted text]

## Fix Plan to Reach [target]

[Numbered list of concrete fixes]

## Verification Summary

[Summary of what was checked]

## Verdict

**Status:** PASS / FAIL
**Rationale:** [brief explanation]
===REVIEW_END===
```

If you find fixable issues, also output FIND/REPLACE pairs:

```
===SECTION_FIX_START===
FILE: /Users/krisztiankoos/projects/learn-ukrainian/curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/the-gender-code.md
FIND:
[exact text from file]
REPLACE:
[corrected text]
---
[... more pairs, max 15 ...]
===SECTION_FIX_END===
```

---

## Boundaries

- Do NOT check factual accuracy against external sources — that is Pass 1's job
- Do NOT score Factual Accuracy dimension — Pass 1 handles that
- Do NOT score generously — honesty prevents bad curriculum
- Do NOT skip any step or dimension
- Do NOT fabricate issues — every critique must cite specific text
- Do NOT reference orchestration artifacts or prior build phases
- Maximum 15 FIND/REPLACE pairs
